item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another one reminding me why I fell in love with this community the first time.

Take me home, whale train.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just trying to imagine what a 100+ car train breaching high into the sky like a steam-powered whale would look like and it is just majestic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wait, dissected… does this mean that the later cars cannot be entered like a normal train?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look at the cutie little train babies…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well after reading everyone's copious feedback I think I understand each side's critical or supportive views of this article. Though, I do have to agree with Kirby and ModernMajorGeneral, its just not that amazing to me as it is to others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do the 4748 instances resemble different real steam locomotives? Like do some look like UP Big Boys, some like an 844, some like a Mallard? Or do they all look the same?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderful, awe inspiring madness. I could read this for hours and hours on end. I love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the best skips I have ever seen. Its adorable, and sentient aquatic trains are a hilarious and amazing idea.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, the first line is a hard-hitter, but the final few lines about 4748-1 are intriguing as hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with you that going out of your way to change your writing to please others is a waste of time, and I don't think one should feel obliged to respond to everyone who dislikes their skips, so I appreciate your response and expression of confidence.

+ show block
– hide block

I also agree with you that adding a site-traditional 'story' has become unhelpful dogma at times and not every article is aided by it, which is why I mentioned complexity (I should probably have said story or complexity). When I say it should have some complexity, I'm thinking of some kind of deeper meaning or message beyond what's directly visible, whether conveyed through a traditional narrative or however. If this sounds pretentious, I would say guilty as charged but also that meaning doesn't have to be particularly elaborate or fancy, or require much deep investment in thought.

My favourite short SCP is SCP-2472, which has nothing resembling a traditional story, but raises the interesting question of 'what if the way the Foundation 'scientifically' assesses the anomalous is fundamentally flawed?', and does so elegantly and simply. For something I've written, SCP-4995 which is also a 'mechanimal', kind of, I also didn't have any real story, but tried to make the point that 'the gig economy is creepy' (not trying to say that my articles are better or that anyone should use them as a model for SCPs, just as a reference for how I think). Both of these are pretty straightforward but there is something left for the reader to think about later.

I don't get that impression from this article. I'm presented with a weird thing in a weird situation, but I'm not left with anything at the end of it. In terms of things that might have made this work for me, I could have been given a message like 'train travel is relaxing' or 'buying a train ticket is irritating' or 'dumping scrap metal into the ocean is bad'. I haven't put enough thought into this to consider how you would work these into the article (or whether the end result would actually be good), but I'm confident you could slip something like that in without hugely reworking the core concept or adding anything like a traditional 'story'. Instead, without being left with a takeaway message (or relatable experience), it's hard for me to care about the article. Sure, here are some weird things, but so?

The caveat is that there are some things that I think stand on their own by simply being a weird thing, and having no further message or implications - but I would argue that they inherently have some level of depth by stating 'there are things on this earth beyond the ability of people to understand'. The issue is that by definition, every single article on the site states this to some extent, so weirdness has to be really weird or originally presented to make this work - which returns back to the point about mechanimals being not very original.

This could just be advice on 'how to write SCPs that I like', and at the end of the day, though, I support the position of doing it your own way, so ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To put it bluntly, the only person's disagreement with you that I personally care about is mine. Cuz I wrote the dang thing.

You raise valid opinions, and I'm responding to you because 1) I know you know what you're talking about 2) I don't want to give off the impression that I'm ignoring people who deign to dislike the skip. So here goes.

holy wall of text batman
had it not been for the laws of this forum

Firstly, okay yes "mechanimals" are admittedly not the most original of starting ingredients. I won't waste time defending the viability of that particular breed of idea here, nor the process that led me to use it. I dare say that comes down to personal taste a fair bit.

This is the sort of article that still needs complexity and story to set it apart.

Here's the line I want to focus on here. I do value your input here, so I'd like to know what you mean by "complexity". That I'm willing to do (and already did, between the initial draft and the current iteration based on feedback - I made it a good 60% longer by describing more whale behavior until I couldn't think of anything else. However, I don't think doing that would help here for you because it'd just be more of the same; refer again to Kirby's post.)

What I refuse to do, at least in this instance, is add a "story" in the site-traditional sense. A combination of dissatisfaction with some of my previous works, and discontentment with aspects of site writing culture has repeatedly sent my motivation to contribute here into mercurial yo-yos of up and down. Long story short, if I'm gonna do this, I'm gonna do it my way (as long as I'm above -10). I'm done sacrificing my creative vision just to please a few more people than before. That has proven to not make me happy in the long run.

If, as Kirby posits, the disconnect is entirely at the core concept of "whale train" and the reader's initial draw, then there exists no possible fix to this article that ends with both of us satisfied. Some people don't like vanilla ice cream no matter what it's served in or what toppings you add, at which point I will respect those opinions but have no mind to do anything about them.

I can add sprinkles or chocolate chips to this scoop if I become convinced that'll improve it. What I won't do is add five scoops of pistachio ice cream and ruin the entire reason I wrote this in the first place.

So, I'll pose to you this question: what twist to the "stock formula", if any, would make this work for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem with this article for me is that the core idea is not particularly original or interesting. While people immediately react with 'wow sea trains, so strange!' the 'machine that acts like animal' (or its sister concept, 'animal that acts like a machine') has been run into the ground such that it has attained 'scary monster' levels of cliche, and is now like a stock-standard SCP template. Whole GOIs basically do this now. While 'machine that acts like animal' can still be done in an interesting way, as can the 'scary monster', there's nothing here to add any twist to the formula. This is the sort of article that still needs complexity and a story to set it apart. Although much of the userbase obviously disagrees with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I find this to be a really interesting point. At least when I read this SCP, I felt like essentially the entire value of this article hinged upon how the reader would react to that opening line. Largely because, while the rest of the article does a good job to give the reader a lot of imagery and explanation of how these sea-trains would work, but it doesn't do much else other than continue to present the idea of sea-trains.

I'm not trying to say that in order for an article to be good it needs a full blown narrative or anything more than an idea. I've had that debate so many times I don't want to have it again.

What I am trying to say, is that a reader can probably judge how much they will enjoy this article from just the first sentence. This you read that and go "Sweet!" then you're in luck because this is about sea-trains and nothing else. However, there are some spoil-sports like me who read that first sentence and say "ok, but so what?" and in that case, I'm unsure what aspect of this article has to offer other than that core idea.

At the end of the day, its different strokes for different folks, but I believe its accurate to say that "the first line of the description" is what makes or breaks an article like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That first line of the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't either but at this point I'm numb to not understanding why people like a certain page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who doesn’t get what’s so great about this skip? ;-;

item.title: Re: Wow!
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Remember to upvote if you liked it :)

item.title: Wow!
item.content:encoded: Amazing Work! Really good idea!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t know about you but I’d swim the opposite direction if I saw a 119 car train in the ocean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There probably could be, I just didn't feel like it.

spoiler
gotcha












They came from the ocean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: while this is interesting and fun as a concept i wonder if there could be a way to subtly hint at some sort of origin of these trainwhales

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And let all the people say "Amen".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not everything needs a story.
Not everything needs development of characters or a moral.
Not everything needs to be incredibly long and full of information.

This is an example why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like the equivalent of modding Thomas the Tank Engine into Skyrim. Changes the look of the dragons, but aside from that there isn't anything interesting. I got all that I needed to understand in the first sentence of the description, most everything else was just stretching the punchline. The bit I found interesting was the animals grafting metal onto themselves from the corpses, that could have been an interesting SCP on its own right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed, thanks to both of you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came here to offer the same suggestion. "…each detached car sheds…" should suffice.

I'd also thought each train produces one offspring the way it was written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps it could be clearer by adding 'each' somewhere in that second sentence? Either "… after 4 days, each of the detached cars shed…"; or '"… their outer layers, each one becoming a new individual instance of …"

I felt like the imagery of whales that are trains was really interesting. Despite how huge they are, whales and other marine mammals are really impressive movers underwater. It was almost a bit of fridge logic that a big long train should actually be much less capable; but trying to imagine it anyway kind of added to the impact, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the end of their lifespan, the last 2-7 train cars detach and float to the surface. The remainder of the train ceases motion and sinks. After approximately 4 days, detached cars shed their outer layers, becoming SCP-4748 instances, and return to their previous depth.

I meant this to mean that the last 2 to 7 cars fall off, and then each individual car becomes a new one. Any idea on how to make that clearer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There ought to be something about population preservation if they have a strict 1.00 fertility rate; violent deaths must universally reduce the standing population.

That said, +1 for cetrainceans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I missing something or is this literally just a train whale with no greater meaning. I like it I'm just confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy the sparseness. It sounds like a fairly recently discovered SCP that hasn't been well-researched yet, and the life cycle gives that a good justification. Could be worth putting in a brief comment about its discovery, though, since the discovery is one of the few things that would be more documented even for a new SCP, and would let you state that it's recent.
The little engines that could. Keep on chugging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the originality and the writing but it does need more meat on its bones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agree with the other comments suggesting that more be added to this article.

I love the silliness of the concept and the tone with which it's discussed. I'm glad it was refreshing to write, as it was also refreshing to read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a rather refreshing SCP to read about after spending time reading about hostile or dangerous ones. I like how it's relatively short and simple yet has an interesting concept: aquatic trains that also function like whales. I especially like how you're able to use the different parts of the train for biological functions so well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not enough anomalous trains around and I think this article stands up as a good argument for having more. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other than the 'train fall' aspect, which I'd have loved to see discussed further, this feels a little too thin conceptually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice catch, fixed now. Thanks, and glad you enjoyed it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, +1.

A minor nitpick: The article says the structure is a 0-6-0 but the source image says that it's a GWR Collett "Castle" Class 4-6-0, which is not a 0-6-0. It's a minor nitpick, but the article's really good.

item.title: Business pitch
item.content:encoded:
So…
Train Whales.
Heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As if Thomas the Tank engine was a whale
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Juvenile SCP-4748 instances often cooperate to line up and attempt to imitate the larger and longer adult instances.

Cute as fuck, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I learned about the existence of whale falls when doing the initial Wikipedia-ing for this one, and it was just too great not to use in this. "Abyssal cetacean carcasses" is just one of those phrases where the words combine to make something more than the sum of its parts, huh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Trains and whales - two great tastes that taste great together!

Thoroughly enjoyed this. If there are future works describing the social organization, sonic communication and possible cultural manifestations of these magnificent creatures, I will probably enjoy those as well.

Full disclosure: I have read as widely as a layperson could about the unique ecology surrounding abyssal cetacean carcasses, which gives some insight into my intellectual tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This looks like you wrote a good one…. it’s nice to see some fresh interesting scp action. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is exactly the kind of short, sweet, surreal thing I lap up. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weryllium pinged me in chat to show off a new article. The first words I read were the description. I fucking love trains and trains scps, and this does its material good service. +1 you magnificent madman.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4748 is the group designation for a species of sentient, aquatic steam locomotives.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the type of skip that j would love to just spend hours reading biology book style logs on, but it just had to be cut short which I am not a fan of. It's unlikely, but I would dig an extended documentation or something.

+1 though, thanks Wery, very cool!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was refreshing to write. Huge thanks to samzeman in for starting the brainstorm in 19 that led me to come up with this. Additionally, thanks to The Great Hippo, Stallmantic, and TheManhattenProject for crit.

Image is from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:5080_Defiant(8267024213).jpg and is CC 2.0.
